column2: Codium tomentosum has been given a nominal abundancev alue of Present for this record as in Arev it had no abundance value. Red algae dominated rocks with abundant corallinaceae and Balanus perforatus. The low lying rocky shelf was incised by a series of narrow deep surge gullies, scoured by pebble. On severly scoured surfaces, Audouinella sp. and Enteromorpha sp. were locally abundant. Undersurfaces also had a low turf of Lomentaria articulata and Callithamnion tetricum. Codium spp. was present in unusually high quantities. Kelp where present was sparse and grazed by abundant Helcion pellucidum.

column2: Porifera indet crusts has been given a nominal abundance value of Present for this record as in Arev it had no abundance value. Whole area surveyed. Upper/midshore typical exposed species. Ls deep pools, overhangs and surge gullies very interesting with a diverse flora and fauna of less commonly faunal species such as Halurus equisetifolius, Asparagopsis armata and superabundant Tubularia indivisa in the surge gullies. An excellent site with more to be found on a better tide.
column3: Rhodophycota indet.(non-calc.crusts) has been given a nominal abundance value of Present for this record as in Arev it had no abundance value. Hard slate with igneous intrusions forming high (to 5m) ridges, with many overhangs and vertical faces. Exposed seaward sides had much Lithophyllum / Lithothamnion, Corallina spp, Barnacles, Actina equina and Nucella lapilus. On the sheltered sides there were many sponges (Halichondria, Hymeniacidon, Grantia) as well as Polysiphonia spp. and other filamentous reds. A large plateau extended beyond the ridges to LWST. Little rock was visible, being covered in encrusting red algae and Mastocarpus stellatus and kelp. The pools contained few boulders but there was an increased diversity of algae and fauna.

column2: Rhodophycota indet.(non-calc.crusts) has been given a nominal abundance value of Present for this record as in Arev it had no abundance value. Hard slate with igneous intrusions forming high (to 5m) ridges, with many overhangs and vertical faces. Exposed seaward sides had much Lithophyllum / Lithothamnion, Corallina spp, Barnacles, Actina equina and Nucella lapilus. On the sheltered sides there were many sponges (Halichondria, Hymeniacidon, Grantia) as well as Polysiphonia spp. and other filamentous reds. A large plateau extended beyond the ridges to LWST. Little rock was visible, being covered in encrusting red algae and Mastocarpus stellatus and kelp. The pools contained few boulders but there was an increased diversity of algae and fauna.
column3: Fucaceae, Nucella, Patella, Trochidae has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. Chthamalus montagui has been given a nominal abundance value of Present for this record as in Arev it had no abundance value. The shore consisted of a fairly level, mica shist which contained many fissures and gullies, backed by mud cliffs. Rockpools had been formed in depressions on the surface. The area is characteristic for barnacle cover and encrusting red algae on drying and non-drying surfaces. Seaweed cover was poor, except at the lower shore where encrusting reds were dominant. The shore appeared to be quite exposed from all southerly directions (SW - ESE). This may make it unsuitable for crabs and other large mobile spp. Cthamalus montagui and Corallina in the pools were characteristic as were Patella, Nucella and Topshells.

column2: Exposed flat rocky shelf with many fissures and gullies. Rockpools formed in depressions. The shore is extremely exposed and this is reflected in the species found. Anemones were abundant as was coralline algae.
column3: Pools dominated by corallinaceae with abundant Ceramium spp. and Patella ulyssiponensis. Most pools shallow, deeper pools with Laminaria digitata, Bifurcaria bifucata and Himanthalia elongata. Mytilus spat formed frequent patches in lower pools. Higher pools with few non coralline algae, dominated by coralline crusts and Patella ulyssiponensis.

column2: Rugged infralittoral slates on a nearshore islet frequented by seabirds. Subzone dominated by Verrucaria maura with frequent patches of Prasiola stipitata. Crevices, fissures and shady areas supported littorinis, Hildenbrandia sp. and occasional barnacles.

column2: Lithophyllum has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. Bedrock of shists and igneous intrusions. Large rocky ridges and , near vertical faces with many overhangs, deep pools and fissures. Actina equina, Barnacles. Nucella lapillus and Patella vulgata were found on the exposed faces. Grantia compressa, Halichondria and Hymeniacion were found in shady places and overhangs with Plumaria elegans. Delesseria was found in shady places in deep pools. Opisthobranch were in fissures, as were many Cancer pagarus and Lipophrys pholis. Ridge adjacent to sandy bay and deep gully parallel to shoreline were surveyed.
column3: Shallow pools in a series of steps with occasional runs of water from one level to the next. Near 100% cover by Corallinacea, Actinia equina abundant as were Patella ulyssiponensis with Enteromorpha sp. attached. Bare rocks with sparse Fucus spiralis, Chthamalus and Littorina neritoides common. Ascophyllum nodosum appearing towards bottom of band.

column3: Porifera has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. Rocky plateau with deep pools, fringed by Corallina, and Coralline crusts covering pool sides. Algae growth in pools sparse, often only on Patella. Deep gullies with vertical and overhanging sides harbouring many species in its fissured and creviced surface. Base of gullies scoured with sand/gravel and rock boulder base. More sheltered rockpools were rich in species and high in abundance. Anemones were common, particularly the beadlet. Sponges abundant. The survey reached MLWST, so only just into the Laminaria zone.

column3: Decapoda, Porifera has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. A broad gully, 10m wide, with coarse sand on bedrock with overlying large slate boulders (to 1.5m). Algae attached to boulders or bedrocks beneath sand. Sand tolerant species in abundance, including Polyides and Furcellaria. Underboulder very rich with near 100% cover by sponges, didemnids and bryozoans, including Diplosoma spongiforme, Trididemnum cereum and Ophlitaspongia seriata. Decapoda well represented as were fish. A rich habitat.

column2: Supralittoral, predominatly upward facing bedrock, dominated by Caloplaca sp. Lecanora atra and indeterminate grey lickens. Ramalina sp. was abundant, particuarly growing out of fissures. There were several pools in this area, comepltely dominated by Enteromorpha.
column3: Fucaceae, Nucella, Patella, Trochidae has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. Chthamalus montagui has been given a nominal abundance value of Present for this record as in Arev it had no abundance value. The shore consisted of a fairly level, mica shist which contained many fissures and gullies, backed by mud cliffs. Rockpools had been formed in depressions on the surface. The area is characteristic for barnacle cover and encrusting red algae on drying and non-drying surfaces. Seaweed cover was poor, except at the lower shore where encrusting reds were dominant. The shore appeared to be quite exposed from all southerly directions (SW - ESE). This may make it unsuitable for crabs and other large mobile spp. Cthamalus montagui and Corallina in the pools were characteristic as were Patella, Nucella and Topshells.

column2: Mid-eulittoral pools in bedrock with afew loose boulders in the bottom. Sides of pools were dominated by Corallinacaea and Corallina officinalis with Mesophyllum lichenoides growing epiphytically. The bottoms of the pools supported a variety of brown algae, including Bifurcaria bifurcata, frequent Halidrs siliquosa and Laminaria digitata. Large tufts of Calliblepharis jubata were the most common red alga. Fuana was sparse, with the pools supporting only anthozoa and an ocasional Gibbula umbilicalis.

column2: Porifera has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. Rocky plateau with deep pools, fringed by Corallina, and Coralline crusts covering pool sides. Algae growth in pools sparse, often only on Patella. Deep gullies with vertical and overhanging sides harbouring many species in its fissured and creviced surface. Base of gullies scoured with sand/gravel and rock boulder base. More sheltered rockpools were rich in species and high in abundance. Anemones were common, particularly the beadlet. Sponges abundant. The survey reached MLWST, so only just into the Laminaria zone.
column3: Rugged infralittoral slates on a nearshore islet frequented by seabirds. Subzone dominated by Verrucaria maura with frequent patches of Prasiola stipitata. Crevices, fissures and shady areas supported littorinids, Hildenbrandia sp. and occasional barnacles.

column2: Cirripedia has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. The upper to mid-shore was a flattish rock plateau with shallow rock pools and gullies. Small amounts of oil residue were found but all the life forms seemed to be healthy. There were large quantities of Corallina to be found and quite a number of Anemonia viridis in evidence. Laurencia was common as were barnacles and limpets. Crabs and starfish were conspicuously absent.
column3: Corallina has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. The rock platform was dissected by gullies running mainly in a SW direction and contained shallow pools with sandy bottoms. Corallina was abundant in the rock pools. The top-mid beach area of incised rock had deep rock pools with cobbles and little else. Many large Thick Topshells , Dogwhelks and eggs were found to be present. Keyhole limpets and cowries were also found. The wide rocky platform extended in both directions from the beach . Mesophyllum lichenoide was found on the corrallina in the shore rockpools.

column2: The shore consisted of a rocky platform with many gullies, rockpools, fissuresj and crevices. A large variety of species were found.

column2: Predominatly upward facing bedrock with Chthamalus spp. and abundant Patella spp. Parts of this area of the shore were shetlered by a high ridge broken by large gullies. There were also large boulder filled pools and small water filled dips recorded seperately. Sparse growths of stunted Fucus vesiculosis var. linearis were scattered throughout this area as were tufts of Lichina pygmaea.

column2: A rugged bedrock plateau with numerous pools, gullies, overhangs and fissures. Restricted algal species and the majority of those present were not abundnant. Rockpools and gullies contained many boulders and some shelly gravel/sand. Actinia equina were abundant as were the corraline algae. In areas of heavy Patella grazing the Barnacles were dominant, particularly on the open, exposed faces. Richest areas for both species and abundance were the sheltered fissures and the vertical faces/overhangs.
column3: Lower eulittoral exposed slates dominated by coralline algae, Patella ulyssiponensis and Mastocarpus stellatus. Patches of Laurencia pinnatifida were heavily grazed and vertical faces were dominated by Balanus perforatus. Small clumps of Ceramium spp., Verrucaria mucosa and Callithamnion sp. were frequent.

column2: Shallow pools in a series of steps with occasional runs of water from one level to the next. Near 100% cover by Corallinacea, Actinia equina abundant as were Patella uyssiponensis with Enteromorpha sp. attached. Bare rocks with sparse Fucus spiralis, Chthamalus and Littorina neritoides common. Ascophyllum nodosum appearing towards bottom of band.

column3: Lithophyllum has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. Bedrock of shists and igneous intrusions. Large rocky ridges and , near vertical faces with many overhangs, deep pools and fissures. Actina equina, Barnacles. Nucella lapillus and Patella vulgata were found on the exposed faces. Grantia compressa, Halichondria and Hymeniacion were found in shady places and overhangs with Plumaria elegans. Delesseria was found in shady places in deep pools. Opisthobranch were in fissures, as were many Cancer pagarus and Lipophrys pholis. Ridge adjacent to sandy bay and deep gully parallel to shoreline were surveyed.

column2: Supralittoral, predominatly upward facing bedrock, dominated by Caloplaca sp. Lecanora atra and indeterminate grey lickens. Ramalina sp. was abundant, particuarly growing out of fissures. There were several pools in this area, comepltely dominated by Enteromorpha.
column3: Corallina has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. The rock platform was dissected by gullies running mainly in a SW direction and contained shallow pools with sandy bottoms. Corallina was abundant in the rock pools. The top-mid beach area of incised rock had deep rock pools with cobbles and little else. Many large Thick Topshells , Dogwhelks and eggs were found to be present. Keyhole limpets and cowries were also found. The wide rocky platform extended in both directions from the beach . Mesophyllum lichenoide was found on the corrallina in the shore rockpools.

column2: Lithophyllum has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. Bedrock of shists and igneous intrusions. Large rocky ridges and , near vertical faces with many overhangs, deep pools and fissures. Actina equina, Barnacles. Nucella lapillus and Patella vulgata were found on the exposed faces. Grantia compressa, Halichondria and Hymeniacion were found in shady places and overhangs with Plumaria elegans. Delesseria was found in shady places in deep pools. Opisthobranch were in fissures, as were many Cancer pagarus and Lipophrys pholis. Ridge adjacent to sandy bay and deep gully parallel to shoreline were surveyed.
column3: Porifera has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. Rocky plateau with deep pools, fringed by Corallina, and Coralline crusts covering pool sides. Algae growth in pools sparse, often only on Patella. Deep gullies with vertical and overhanging sides harbouring many species in its fissured and creviced surface. Base of gullies scoured with sand/gravel and rock boulder base. More sheltered rockpools were rich in species and high in abundance. Anemones were common, particularly the beadlet. Sponges abundant. The survey reached MLWST, so only just into the Laminaria zone.

column2: Ramalina siliquosa, Caloplaca has been given a nominal abundance value of Present for this record as in Arev it had no abundance value. Supralittoral lichens were noted from over 40 metres above the shore, particularly Xanthoria parietina and Ramalina siliquosa, though only the 4-8m ASL band was surveyed. Abundance was high though diversity was not great. Grey lichens abundant.
column3: Codium tomentosum has been given a nominal abundance value of Present for this record as in Arev it had no abundance value. Red algae dominated rocks with abundant corallinaceae and Balanus perforatus. The low lying rocky shelf was incised by a series of narrow deep surge gullies, scoured by pebble. On severly scoured surfaces, Audouinella sp. and Enteromorpha sp. were locally abundant. Undersurfaces also had a low turf of Lomentaria articulata and Callithamnion tetricum. Codium spp. was present in unusually high quantities. Kelp where present was sparse and grazed by abundant Helcion pellucidum.

column2: Porifera indet crusts has been given a nominal abundance value of Present for this record as in Arev it had no abundance value. Whole area surveyed. Upper/midshore typical exposed species. Ls deep pools, overhangs and surge gullies very interesting with a diverse flora and fauna of less commonly faunal species such as Halurus equisetifolius, Asparagopsis armata and superabundant Tubularia indivisa in the surge gullies. An excellent site with more to be found on a better tide.
column3: The shore consisted of a rocky platform with many gullies, rockpools, fissures and crevices. A large variety of species were found.

column2: Lithophyllum has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. column3: Lower eulittoral exposed slates dominated by coralline algae, Patella ulyssiponensis and Mastocarpus stellatus. Patches of Laurencia pinnatifida were heavily grazed and vertical faces were dominated by Balanus perforatus. Small clumps of Ceramium spp., Verrucaria mucosa and Callithamnion sp. were frequent.

column2: Porifera indet crusts has been given a nominal abundance value of Present for this record as in Arev it had no abundance value. Whole area surveyed. Upper/midshore typical exposed species. column3: Predominatly upward facing bedrock with Chthamalus spp. and abundant Patella spp. Parts of this area of the shore were sheltered by a high ridge broken by large gullies. There were also large boulder filled pools and small water filled dips recorded seperately. Sparse growths of stunted Fucus vesiculosis var. linearis were scattered throughout this area as were tufts of Lichina pygmaea.

column2: Porphyra has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. A good zonation of Chthamalus dominated rock with Pelvetia canaliculata and frequent patches of Lichina pygmaea. Above this, barnacles disappeared between a band of Porphyra umbilicalis and P.linearis. Occasional pools were shallow with filamentous green algae. Prasoila stipitata formed a distinct band above Porphyra and below Verrucaria maura.

column2: Decapoda, Porifera has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. A broad gully, 10m wide, with coarse sand on bedrock with overlying large slate boulders (to 1.5m). Algae attached to boulders or bedrocks beneath sand. Sand tolerant species in abundance, including Polyides and Furcellaria. Underboulder very rich with near 100% cover by sponges, didemnids and bryozoans, including Diplosoma spongiforme, Trididemnum cereum and Ophlitaspongia seriata. Decapoda well represented as were fish. A rich habitat.
column3: Very exposed shore consisting of a rock platform with gullies and rockpools. A large variety of species was found and the area was abundant in oarweed. The survey was incomplete.

column2: Rhodophycota indet.(non-calc.crusts) has been given a nominal abundance value of Present for this record as in Arev it had no abundance value. Hard slate with igneous intrusions forming high (to 5m) ridges, with many overhangs and vertical faces. Exposed seaward sides had much Lithophyllum / Lithothamnion, Corallina spp, Barnacles, Actina equina and Nucella lapilus. On the sheltered sides there were many sponges (Halichondria, Hymeniacidon, Grantia) as well as Polysiphonia spp. and other filamentous reds. A large plateau extended beyond the ridges to LWST. Little rock was visible, being covered in encrusting red algae and Mastocarpus stellatus and kelp. The pools contained few boulders but there was an increased diversity of algae and fauna.
column3: Shallow pools in a series of steps with occasional runs of water from one level to the next. Near 100% cover by Corallinacea, Actinia equina abundant as were Patella ulyssiponensis with Enteromorpha sp. attached. Bare rocks with sparse Fucus spiralis, Chthamalus and Littorina neritoides common. Ascophyllum nodosum appearing towards bottom of band.

column2: Pools dominated by corallinaceae with abundant Ceramium spp. and Patella ulyssiponensis. Most pools shallow, deeper pools with Laminaria digitata, Bifurcaria bifucata and Himanthalia leongata. Mytilus spat formed frequent patches in lower pools. Higher pools with few non coralline algae, dominated by coralline crusts and Patella ulyssiponensis.
column3: Pools dominated by corallinaceae with abundant Ceramium spp. and Patella ulyssiponensis. Most pools shallow, deeper pools with Laminaria digitata, Bifurcaria bifucata and Himanthalia elongata. Mytilus spat formed frequent patches in lower pools. Higher pools with few non coralline algae, dominated by coralline crusts and Patella ulyssiponensis.

column3: Mid-eulittoral pools in bedrock with afew loose boulders in the bottom. Sides of pools were dominated by Corallinacaea and Corallina officinalis with Mesophyllum lichenoides growing epiphytically. The bottoms of the pools supported a variety of brown algae, including Bifurcaria bifurcata, frequent Halidrs siliquosa and Laminaria digitata. Large tufts of Calliblepharis jubata were the most common red alga. Fuana was sparse, with the pools supporting only anthozoa and an occasional Gibbula umbilicalis.

column3: Fucaceae, Nucella, Patella, Trochidae has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. Chthamalus montagui has been given a nominal abundance value of Present for this record as in Arev it had no abundance value. The shore consisted of a fairly level, mica shist which contained many fissures and gullies, backed by mud cliffs. Rockpools had been formed in depressions on the surface. The area is characteristic for barnacle cover and encrusting red algae on drying and non-drying surfaces. Seaweed cover was poor, except at the lower shore where encrusting reds were dominant. The shore appeared to be quite exposed from all southerly directions (SW - ESE). This may make it unsuitable for crabs and other large mobile spp. Cthamalus montagui and Corallina in the pools were characteristic as were Patella, Nucella and Topshells.

column2: Rugged slate bedrock at the top of an islet. Site used heavily by roosting seabirds and guano was abundant. Lichens restricted to vertical bedrock with reduced guano load.
column3: A rugged bedrock plateau with numerous pools, gullies, overhangs and fissures. Restricted algal species and the majority of those present were not abundnant. Rockpools and gullies contained many boulders and some shelly gravel/sand. Actinia equina were abundant as were the corraline algae. In areas of heavy Patella grazing the Barnacles were dominant, particularly on the open, exposed faces. Richest areas for both species and abundance were the sheltered fissures and the vertical faces/overhangs.

column3: Porifera indet crusts has been given a nominal abundance value of Present for this record as in Arev it had no abundance value. Whole area surveyed. Upper/midshore typical exposed species. Ls deep pools, overhangs and surge gullies very interesting with a diverse flora and fauna of less commonly faunal species such as Halurus equisetifolius, Asparagopsis armata and superabundant Tubularia indivisa in the surge gullies. An excellent site with more to be found on a better tide.

column2: Rhodophycota indet.(non-calc.crusts) has been given a nominal abundance value of Present for this record as in Arev it had no abundance value. Hard slate with igneous intrusions forming high (to 5m) ridges, with many overhangs and vertical faces. Exposed seaward sides had much Lithophyllum / Lithothamnion, Corallina spp, Barnacles, Actina equina and Nucella lapilus. On the sheltered sides there were many sponges (Halichondria, Hymenigacidon, Grantia) as well as Polysiphonia spp. and other filamentous reds. A large plateau extended beyond the ridges to LWST. Little rock was visible, being covered in encrusting red algae and Mastocarpus stellatus and kelp. The pools contained few boulders but there was an increased diversity of algae and fauna.

column2: Rugged lower eulittoral bedrock with dense Mastocarpus stellatus interspersed with himanthalia elongata. Upper faces formrd very flat platforms with abundant and varied algal cover, predominantly reds, with Cladophora sp. and occasional Fucas serratus, with Patella ulyssiponensis and Gibbula umbilicalis. Overhangs held a aried fauna includingGrantia compressa and a variety of sponges, together with frequent Aplidium punctum and Botrylus schlosseri.

column2: Decapoda, Porifera has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. A broad gully, 10m wide, with coarse sand on bedrock with overlying large slate boulders (to 1.5m). Algae attached to boulders or bedrocks beneath sand. Sand tolerant species in abundance, including Polyides and Furcellaria. Underboulder very rich with near 100% cover by sponges, didemnids and bryozoans, including Diplosoma spongiforme, Trididemnum cereum and Ophlitaspongia seriata. Decapoda well represented as were fish. A rich habitat.
column3: Corallina has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. The rock platform was dissected by gullies running mainly in a SW direction and contained shallow pools with sandy bottoms. Corallina was abundant in the rock pools. The top-mid beach area of incised rock had deep rock pools with cobbles and little else. Many large Thick Topshells , Dogwhelks and eggs were found to be present. Keyhole limpets and cowries were also found. The wide rocky platform extended in both directions from the beach . Mesophyllum lichenoide was found on the corrallina in the shore rockpools.

column3: Mid-eulittoral pools in bedrock with afew loose boulders in the bottom. Sides of pools were dominated by Corallinacaea and Corallina officinalis with Mesophyllum lichenoides growing epiphytically. The bottoms of the pools supported a variety of brown algae, including Bifurcaria bifurcata, frequent Halidrs siliquosa and Laminaria digitata. Large tufts of Calliblepharis jubata were the most common red alga. Fuana was sparse, with the pools supporting only anthozoa and an occasional Gibbula umbilicalis.

column2: Codium tomentosum has been given a nominal abundance value of Present for this record as in Arev it had no abundance alue. Red algae dominated rocks with abundant corallinaceae and Balanus perforatus. The low lying rocky shelf was incised by a series of narrow deep surge gullies, scoured by pebble. On severly scoured surfaces, Audouinella sp. and Enteromorpha sp. were locally abundant. Undersurfaces also had a low turf of Lomentaria articulata and Callithamnion tetricum. Codium spp. was present in unusually high quantities. Kelp where present was sparse and grazed by abundant Helcion pellucidum.

column2: Pools dominated by corallinaceae with abundant Ceramium spp. and Patella ulyssiponensis. Most pools shallow, deeper pools with Laminaria digitata, Bifurcaria bifucata and Himanthalia elongata. Mytilus spat formed frequent patches in lower pools. Higher pools with few non coralline algae, dominated by coralline crusts and Patella ulyssiponensis.
column3: A rugged bedrock plateau with numerous pools, gullies, overhangs and fissures. Restricted algal species and the majority of those present were not abundnant. Rockpools and gullies contained many boulders and some shelly gravel/sand. Actinia equina were abundant as were the corraline algae. In areas of heavy Patella grazing the Barnacles were dominant, particularly on the open, exposed faces. Richest areas for both species and abundance were the sheltered fissures and the vertical faces/overhangs.

column2: Lithophyllum has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. Bedrock of shists and igneous intrusions. Large rocky ridges and , near vertical faces with many overhangs, deep pools and fissures. Actina equina, Barnacles. Nucella lapillus and Patella vulgata were found on the exposed faces. Grantia compressa, Halichondria and Hymeniacion were found in shady places and overhangs with Plumaria elegans. Delesseria was found in shady places in deep pools. Opisthobranch were in fissures, as were many Cancer pagarus and Lipophrys pholis. Ridge adjacent to sandy bay and deep gully parallel to shoreline were surveyed.
column3: Semibalanus balanoides dominated rock with frequent Balanus perforatus and abundant Patella vulgata. Small red algae on Patella, including Plocamium cartilagineum and Ceramium spp.. Fucus vesiculosus forma linearis was common forming a band from the lower eulittoral up into the lower littoral fringe. Mytilus edulis spat formed a scattered turf.

There were large quantities of Corallina to be found and quite a number of Anemonia viridis in evidence. Laurencia was common as were barnacles and limpets. Crabs and starfish were conspicuously absent.

Chthamalus montagui has been given a nominal abundance value of Present for this record as in Arev it had no abundance value. The shore consisted of a fairly level, mica shist which contained many fissures and gullies, backed by mud cliffs. Rockpools had been formed in depressions on the surface. The area is characteristic for barnacle cover and encrusting red algae on drying and non-drying surfaces. Seaweed cover was poor, except at the lower shore where encrusting reds were dominant. The shore appeared to be quite exposed from all southerly directions (SW - ESE). This may make it unsuitable for crabs and other large mobile spp. Cthamalus montagui and Corallina in the pools were characteristic as were Patella, Nucella and Topshells.

column2: Mid-eulittoral pools in bedrock with afew loose boulders in the botom. Sides of pools were dominated by Corallinacaea and Corallina officinalis with Mesophyllum lichenoides growing epiphytically. The bottoms of the pools supported a variety of brown algae, including Bifurcaria bifurcata, frequent Halidrs siliquosa and Laminaria digitata. Large tufts of Calliblepharis jubata were the most common red alga. Fuana was sparse, with the pools supporting only anthozoa and an occasional Gibbula umbilicalis.

column2: Porifera has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. Rocky plateau with deep pools, fringed by Corallina, and Coralline crusts covering pool sides. Algae growth in pools sparse, often only on Patella. Deep gullies with vertical and overhanging sides harbouring many species in its fissured and creviced surface. Base of gullies scoured with sand/gravel and rock boulder base. More sheltered rockpools were rich in species and high in abundance. Anemones were common, particularly the beadlet. Sponges abundant. The survey reached MLWST, so only just into the Laminaria zone.
column3: Decapoda, Porifera has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. A broad gully, 10m wide, with coarse sand on bedrock with overlying large slate boulders (to 1.5m). Algae attached to boulders or bedrocks beneath sand. Sand tolerant species in abundance, including Polyides and Furcellaria. Underboulder very rich with near 100% cover by sponges, didemnids and bryozoans, including Diplosoma spongiforme, Trididemnum cereum and Ophlitaspongia seriata. Decapoda well represented as were fish. A rich habitat.

column2: Codium tomentosum haqs been given a nominal abundance value of Present for this record as in Arev it had no abundance value. Red algae dominated rocks with abundant corallinaceae and Balanus perforatus. The low lying rocky shelf was incised by a series of narrow deep surge gullies, scoured by pebble. On severly scoured surfaces, Audouinella sp. and Enteromorpha sp. were locally abundant. Undersurfaces also had a low turf of Lomentaria articulata and Callithamnion tetricum. Codium spp. was present in unusually high quantities. Kelp where present was sparse and grazed by abundant Helcion pellucidum.

Hard slate with igneous intrusions forming high (to 5m) ridges, with many overhangs and vertical faces. Exposed seaward sides had much Lithophyllum / Lithothamnion, Corallina spp, Barnacles, Actina equina and Nucella lapilus. On the sheltered sides there were many sponges (Halichondria, Hymeniacidon, Grantia) as well as Polysiphonia spp. and other filamentous reds. A large plateau extended beyond the ridges to LWST. column3: Lithophyllum has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. Bedrock of shists and igneous intrusions. Large rocky ridges and , near vertical faces with many overhangs, deep pools and fissures. Actina equina, Barnacles. Nucella lapillus and Patella vulgata were found on the exposed faces. Grantia compressa, Halichondria and Hymeniacion were found in shady places and overhangs with Plumaria elegans. Delesseria was found in shady places in deep pools. Opisthobranch were in fissures, as were many Cancer pagarus and Lipophrys pholis. Ridge adjacent to sandy bay and deep gully parallel to shoreline were surveyed.

column2: Rhodophycota indet.(non-calc.crusts) has been given a nominal abundance value of Present for this recbord as in Arev it had no abundance value. Hard slate with igneous intrusions forming high (to 5m) ridges, with many overhangs and vertical faces. Exposed seaward sides had much Lithophyllum / Lithothamnion, Corallina spp, Barnacles, Actina equina and Nucella lapilus. On the sheltered sides there were many sponges (Halichondria, Hymeniacidon, Grantia) as well as Polysiphonia spp. and other filamentous reds. A large plateau extended beyond the ridges to LWST. Little rock was visible, being covered in encrusting red algae and Mastocarpus stellatus and kelp. The pools contained few boulders but there was an increased diversity of algae and fauna.

column2: Codium tomentosum has been given a nominal abundance value of Persent for this record as in Arev it had no abundance value. Red algae dominated rocks with abundant corallinaceae and Balanus perforatus. The low lying rocky shelf was incised by a series of narrow deep surge gullies, scoured by pebble. On severly scoured surfaces, Audouinella sp. and Enteromorpha sp. were locally abundant. Undersurfaces also had a low turf of Lomentaria articulata and Callithamnion tetricum. Codium spp. was present in unusually high quantities. Kelp where present was sparse and grazed by abundant Helcion pellucidum.

column3: Porifera has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. Rocky plateau with deep pools, fringed by Corallina, and Coralline crusts covering pool sides. Algae growth in pools sparse, often only on Patella. Deep gullies with vertical and overhanging sides harbouring many species in its fissured and creviced surface. Base of gullies scoured with sand/gravel and rock boulder base. More sheltered rockpools were rich in species and high in abundance. Anemones were common, particularly the beadlet. Sponges abundant. The survey reached MLWST, so only just into the Laminaria zone.

column2: Fissured rock with superabundant Littorina saxatilis. Pools shallow with Enteromorpha sp. and black blobs, plus other green algae. Gibbula umbilicalis occasional on rocks and Chthamalus rare.
column3: Littorininae has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. Rugged infralittoral slates dominated by Porphyra umbilicalis on Verrucaria maura and scattered Chthamalus spp. Damp patches and crevices supported littorinids and Hildenbrandia sp. Small pools held Tigriopus brevicornis.

column2: Porifera indet crusts has been given a nominal abundance value of Present for this record as in Arev it had no abundance value. Whole area surveyed. Upper/midshore typical exposed species. Ls deep pools, overhangs and surge gullies very interesting with a diverse flora and fauna of less commonly faunal species such as Halurus equisetifolius, Asparagopsis armata and superabundatn Tubularia indivisa in the surge gullies. An excellent site with more to be found on a better tide.
column3: Porifera indet crusts has been given a nominal abundance value of Present for this record as in Arev it had no abundance value. Whole area surveyed. Upper/midshore typical exposed species. column2: Patella ulyssiponensis has been given a nominal abundance value of Present for this record as in Arev it had no abundance value. Shallow gully rockpools in the mid eulittoral were dominated by Corallinaceae and Corallina officinalis with superabundant Patella ulyssiponensis. Fissures held Lipophrys pholis and Sagartia elegans. Limpet shells were covered in Enteromorpha spp.
column3: Abundant vertical faces and overhangs of scoured surge gullies and rockmills were dominated by nearly 100% cover of Balanus perforatus. Actinia equina were usually present in large numbers and there was abundant coralline crusts. Halichondria panicea and Hymeniacidon perleve were common, and a variety of red algae, including Lomentaria articulata and Plumaria elegans were frequent.

column2: Ramalina siliquosa, Caloplaca has been given a nominal abundvance value of Present for this record as in Arev it had no abundance value. Supralittoral lichens were noted from over 40 metres above the shore, particularly Xanthoria parietina and Ramalina siliquosa, though only the 4-8m ASL band was surveyed. Abundance was high though diversity was not great. Grey lichens abundant.

On severly scoured surfaces, Audouinella sp. and Enteromorpha sp. were locally abundant. Undersurfaces also had a low turf of Lomentaria articulata and Callithamnion tetricum. Codium spp. was present in unusually high quantities. Kelp where present was sparse and grazed by abundant Helcion pellucidum.

column2: Ramalina siliquosa, Caloplaca has been given a nominal abundance value of Present for this record as in Arev it had no abundance value. Supralittoral lichens were noted from over 40 metres above the shore, particularly Xanthoria parietina and Ramalina siliquosa, though only the 4-8m ASL band was surveyed. Abundance was high though diversity was not great. Grey lichens abundant.
column3: Porphyra has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. A good zonation of Chthamalus dominated rock with Pelvetia canaliculata and frequent patches of Lichina pygmaea. Above this, barnacles disappeared between a band of Porphyra umbilicalis and P.linearis. Occasional pools were shallow with filamentous green algae. Prasiola stipitata formed a distinct band above Porphyra and below Verrucaria maura.

column3: A rugged bedrock plateau with numerous pools, gullies, overhangs and fissures. Restricted algal species and the majority of those present were not abundnant. column2: Rhodophycota indet.(non-calc.crusts) has been given a nominal abundance value of Present for this record as in Arev it had no abundance value. Hard slate with igneous intrusions forming high (to 5m) ridges, with many overhangs and vertical faces. Exposed seaward sides had much Lithophyllum / Lithothamnion, Corallina spp, Barnacles, Actina equina andm Nucella lapilus. On the sheltered sides there were many sponges (Halichondria, Hymeniacidon, Grantia) as well as Polysiphonia spp. and other filamentous reds. A large plateau extended beyond the ridges to LWST. Little rock was visible, being covered in encrusting red algae and Mastocarpus stellatus and kelp. The pools contained few boulders but there was an increased diversity of algae and fauna.

column2: Rugged slate bedrock at the top of an islet. Site used heavily by roosting seabirds and guano was abundant. Lichens restricted to vertical bedrock with reduced guano load.
column3: Corallina has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. The rock platform was dissected by gullies running mainly in a SW direction and contained shallow pools with sandy bottoms. Corallina was abundant in the rock pools. The top-mid beach area of incised rock had deep rock pools with cobbles and little else. Many large Thick Topshells , Dogwhelks and eggs were found to be present. Keyhole limpets and cowries were also found. The wide rocky platform extended in both directions from the beach . Mesophyllum lichenoide was found on the corrallina in the shore rockpools.

column2: Porifera has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. Rocky plateau with deep pools, fringed by Corallina, and Coralline crusts covering pool sides. Algae growth in pools sparse, often only on Patella. Deep gullies with vertical and overhanging sides harbouring many species in its fissured and creviced surface. Base of gullies scoured with sand/gravel and rock boulder base. More sheltered rockpools were rich in species and high in abundance. Anemones were common, particularly the beadlet. Sponges abundant. The survey reached MLWST, so only just into the Laminaria zone.

column2: Abundant vertical faces and overhangs of scoured surge gullies and rockmills were dominated by nearly 100% cover of Balanus perforatus. Actinia equina were usually present in large numbers and there was abundant coralline crusts. Halichondria panicea and Hymeniacidon perleve were common, and a variety of red algae, including Lomentaria articulata and Plumaria elegans were frequent.
column3: Lithophyllum has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. Bedrock of shists and igneous intrusions. Large rocky ridges and , near vertical faces with many overhangs, deep pools and fissures. Actina equina, Barnacles. Nucella lapillus and Patella vulgata were found on the exposed faces. Grantia compressa, Halichondria and Hymeniacion were found in shady places and overhangs with Plumaria elegans. Delesseria was found in shady places in deep pools. Opisthobranch were in fissures, as were many Cancer pagarus and Lipophrys pholis. Ridge adjacent to sandy bay and deep gully parallel to shoreline were surveyed.

column3: The shore consisted of a rocky platform with many gullies, rockpools, fissures and crevices. A large variety of species were found.

column2: Decapoda, Porifera has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. column2: Exposed flat rocky shelf with many fissures and gulliezs. Rockpools formed in depressions. The shore is extremely exposed and this is reflected in the species found. Anemones were abundant as was coralline algae.

column2: Codium tomentosum has been given a nominal abuyndance value of Present for this record as in Arev it had no abundance value. Red algae dominated rocks with abundant corallinaceae and Balanus perforatus. The low lying rocky shelf was incised by a series of narrow deep surge gullies, scoured by pebble. On severly scoured surfaces, Audouinella sp. and Enteromorpha sp. were locally abundant. Undersurfaces also had a low turf of Lomentaria articulata and Callithamnion tetricum. Codium spp. was present in unusually high quantities. Kelp where present was sparse and grazed by abundant Helcion pellucidum.

column3: Porifera has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. Rocky plateau with deep pools, fringed by Corallina, and Coralline crusts covering pool sides. Algae growth in pools sparse, often only on Patella. Deep gullies with vertical and overhanging sides harbouring many species in its fissured and creviced surface. Base of gullies scoured with sand/gravel and rock boulder base. More sheltered rockpools were rich in species and high in abundance. Anemones were common, particularly the beadlet. Sponges abundant. The survey reached MLWST, so only just into the Laminaria zone.

column2: Fucaceae, Nucella, Patella, Trochidae has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. Chthamalus montagui has been given a nominal abundance value of Present for this record as in Arev it had no abundance value. The shore consisted of a fairly level, mica shist which containbed many fissures and gullies, backed by mud cliffs. Rockpools had been formed in depressions on the surface. The area is characteristic for barnacle cover and encrusting red algae on drying and non-drying surfaces. Seaweed cover was poor, except at the lower shore where encrusting reds were dominant. The shore appeared to be quite exposed from all southerly directions (SW - ESE). This may make it unsuitable for crabs and other large mobile spp. Cthamalus montagui and Corallina in the pools were characteristic as were Patella, Nucella and Topshells.

column2: Pools dominated by corallinaceae with abundant Ceramium spp. and Patella ulyssiponensis. Most pools shallow, deeper pools with Laminaria digitata, Bifurcaria bifucata and Himanthalia elongata. Mytilus spat formed frequent patches in lower pools. Higher pools with few non coralline algae, dominated by coralline crusts and Patella ulyssiponensis.
column3: Fissured rock with superabundant Littorina saxatilis. Pools shallow with Enteromorpha sp. and black blobs, plus other green algae. Gibbula umbilicalis occasional on rocks and Chthamalus rare.

column2: Ramalina siliquosa, Caloplaca has been given a nominal abundqance value of Present for this record as in Arev it had no abundance value. Supralittoral lichens were noted from over 40 metres above the shore, particularly Xanthoria parietina and Ramalina siliquosa, though only the 4-8m ASL band was surveyed. Abundance was high though diversity was not great. Grey lichens abundant.

column3: Slate and boulder filled gully just below shingle, running down the shore with through flowing water. Algae abundant, particularly Ceramium sp., Ulva and Chondrus etc. Sargassum in pools. Boulders with restricted underboulder community, relatively mobile and dominated by Spirorbidae. Corallinacea abundant, rock surfaces above water with abundant Fucoid and Ascophyllum growth.

column2: Supralittoral, predominatly upward facing bedrock, dominated by Caloplaca sp. Lecanora atra and indeterminate grey lickens. Ramalina sp. was abundant, particuarly growing out of fissures. There were several pools in this area, comepltely dominated by Enteromorpha.
column3: Lithophyllum has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. Bedrock of shists and igneous intrusions. Large rocky ridges and , near vertical faces with many overhangs, deep pools and fissures. Actina equina, Barnacles. Nucella lapillus and Patella vulgata were found on the exposed faces. Grantia compressa, Halichondria and Hymeniacion were found in shady places and overhangs with Plumaria elegans. Delesseria was found in shady places in deep pools. Opisthobranch were in fissures, as were many Cancer pagarus and Lipophrys pholis. Ridge adjacent to sandy bay and deep gully parallel to shoreline were surveyed.

column2: A rugged bedrock plateau with numerous pools, gullies, overhangs and fissures. Restricted algal species and the majority of those present were not abundnant. In areas of heavy Patella grazing the Barnacles were dominant, particularly on the open, exposed faces. Richest areas for both species and abundance were the sheltered fissures and the vertical faces/overhangs.

column2: Lower eulittoral exposed slates dominated by coralline algae, Patella ulyssiponensis and Mastocarpus stellatus. Patches of Laurencia pinnaitfida were heavily grazed and vertical faces were dominated by Balanus perforatus. Small clumps of Ceramium spp., Verrucaria mucosa and Callithamnion sp. were frequent.
column3: Lower eulittoral exposed slates dominated by coralline algae, Patella ulyssiponensis and Mastocarpus stellatus. Patches of Laurencia pinnatifida were heavily grazed and vertical faces were dominated by Balanus perforatus. Small clumps of Ceramium spp., Verrucaria mucosa and Callithamnion sp. were frequent.

column2: Rugged slate bedrock at the top of an islet. Site used heavily by roosting seabirds and guano was abundant. Lichens restricted to vertical bedrock with reduced guano load.
column3: Corallina has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. The rock platform was dissected by gullies running mainly in a SW direction and contained shallow pools with sandy bottoms. Corallina was abundant in the rock pools. The top-mid beach area of incised rock had deep rock pools with cobbles and little else. Many large Thick Topshells , Dogwhelks and eggs were found to be present. Keyhole limpets and cowries were also found. The wide rocky platform extended in both directions from the beach . Mesophyllum lichenoide was found on the corrallina in the shore rockpools.

column2: Supralittoral, predominatly upward facing bedrock, dominated by Caloplaca sp. Lecanora atra and indeterminate grey lickens. Ramalina sp. was abundant, particuarly growing out of fissures. There were several pools in this area, comepltely dominated by Enteromorpha.
column3: Codium tomentosum has been given a nominal abundance value of Present for this record as in Arev it had no abundance value. Red algae dominated rocks with abundant corallinaceae and Balanus perforatus. The low lying rocky shelf was incised by a series of narrow deep surge gullies, scoured by pebble. On severly scoured surfaces, Audouinella sp. and Enteromorpha sp. were locally abundant. Undersurfaces also had a low turf of Lomentaria articulata and Callithamnion tetricum. Codium spp. was present in unusually high quantities. Kelp where present was sparse and grazed by abundant Helcion pellucidum.

column2: Decapoda, Porifera has been removed from the psecies list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. A broad gully, 10m wide, with coarse sand on bedrock with overlying large slate boulders (to 1.5m). Algae attached to boulders or bedrocks beneath sand. Sand tolerant species in abundance, including Polyides and Furcellaria. Underboulder very rich with near 100% cover by sponges, didemnids and bryozoans, including Diplosoma spongiforme, Trididemnum cereum and Ophlitaspongia seriata. Decapoda well represented as were fish. A rich habitat.

column2: Shallow pools in a series of steps with occasional runs of water from one level to the next. column2: Patella ulyssiponensis has been given a nominal abundance value of Present for this record as in Arev it had no abundance value. Shallow gully rockpools in the mid eulittoral were dominated by Corallinaceae and Corallina officinalis with superabundant Patella ulyssiponensis. Fissures held Lipophrys pholis and Sagartia elegans. Limpet shells were covered in Enteromorpha spp.
column3: Mid-eulittoral pools in bedrock with afew loose boulders in the bottom. Sides of pools were dominated by Corallinacaea and Corallina officinalis with Mesophyllum lichenoides growing epiphytically. The bottoms of the pools supported a variety of brown algae, including Bifurcaria bifurcata, frequent Halidrs siliquosa and Laminaria digitata. Large tufts of Calliblepharis jubata were the most common red alga. Fuana was sparse, with the pools supporting only anthozoa and an occasional Gibbula umbilicalis.

column2: Porifera indet crusts has been given a nominal abundance value of Present for this record as in Arev it had no abundance value. Whole area surveyed. Upper/midshore typical exposed species. Ls deep pools, overhangs and surge gullies very interesting with a diverse flora and fauna of less commonly faunal species such as Halurus equisetifolius, Asparagopsis armata and superabundant Tubularia indivisa in the surge gullies. An excellent site with more to be found on a better tide.
column3: Porifera has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. Rocky plateau with deep pools, fringed by Corallina, and Coralline crusts covering pool sides. Algae growth in pools sparse, often only on Patella. Deep gullies with vertical and overhanging sides harbouring many species in its fissured and creviced surface. Base of gullies scoured with sand/gravel and rock boulder base. More sheltered rockpools were rich in species and high in abundance. Anemones were common, particularly the beadlet. Sponges abundant. The survey reached MLWST, so only just into the Laminaria zone.

column3: Littorina littorea has been given a nominal abundance value of Present for this record as in Arev it had no abundance value. Small, shallow pools in dips in the bedrock. Dominated by Coralline crusts and Corallina officinalis, with frequent Dumontia contorta. Actinia equina were frequent on the sides of the pools, as they were on the surrounding bedrock.

column2: Cirripedia has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. The upper to mid-shore was a flattish rock plateau with shallow rock pools and gullies. Small amounts of oil residue were found but all the life forms seemed to be healthy. There were large quantities of Corallina to be found and quite a number of Anemonia viridis in evidence. Laurencia was common as were barnacles and limpets. Crabs and starfish were conspicuously absent.

column2: Ramalina siliquosa has been given a nominal abundance value of Present for this record as in Arev it had no abundance value. One of a few rock outcrops which reach through the uppershore sand and shingle to reach the cliff base at 6-7m ACD. Lichen dominated though not particularly well covered, Ramalina siliquosa short and stubby. Green lichens occasional.
column3: Rugged lower eulittoral bedrock with dense Mastocarpus stellatus interspersed with himanthalia elongata. Upper faces formrd very flat platforms with abundant and varied algal cover, predominantly reds, with Cladophora sp. and occasional Fucas serratus, with Patella ulyssiponensis and Gibbula umbilicalis. Overhangs held a varied fauna includingGrantia compressa and a variety of sponges, together with frequent Aplidium punctum and Botrylus schlosseri.

column2: Fucaceae, Nucella, Patella, Trochidae has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. Chthamalus montagui has been given a nominal abundance value of Present for this record as in Arev it had no abundance value. The shore consisted of a fairly level, mica shist which contained many fissures and gullies, backed by mud cliffs. Rockpools had been formed in depressions on the surface. The area is characteristic for barnacle cover and encrusting red algae on drying and non-drying surfaces. Seaweed cover was poor, except at the lower shore where encrusting reds were dominant. The shore appeared to be quite exposed from all southerl directions (SW - ESE). This may make it unsuitable for crabs and other large mobile spp. Cthamalus montagui and Corallina in the pools were characteristic as were Patella, Nucella and Topshells.

column2: Lithophyllum has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. Bedrock of shists and igneous intrusions. Large rocky ridges and , near vertical faces with many overhangs, deep pools and fissures. Actina equina, Barnacles. Nucella lapillus and Patella vulgata were found on the exposed faces. Grantia compressa, Halichondria and Hymeniacion were found in shady places and overhangs with Plumaria elegans. Delesseria was found in shady places in deep pools. Opisthobranch were in fissures, as were many Cancer pagarus and Lipophrys pholis. Ridge adjacent to sandy bay and deep gully parallel to shoreline were surveyed.
column3: Fucaceae, Nucella, Patella, Trochidae has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. Chthamalus montagui has been given a nominal abundance value of Present for this record as in Arev it had no abundance value. The shore consisted of a fairly level, mica shist which contained many fissures and gullies, backed by mud cliffs. Rockpools had been formed in depressions on the surface. The area is characteristic for barnacle cover and encrusting red algae on drying and non-drying surfaces. Seaweed cover was poor, except at the lower shore where encrusting reds were dominant. The shore appeared to be quite exposed from all southerly directions (SW - ESE). This may make it unsuitable for crabs and other large mobile spp. Cthamalus montagui and Corallina in the pools were characteristic as were Patella, Nucella and Topshells.

column2: Rhodophycota indet.(non-calc.crusts) has been given a nominal abundance value of Present for this record as in Arev it had no abundance value. Hard slate with igneous intrusions forming high (to 5m) ridges, with many overhangs and vertical faces. Exposed seaward sides had much Lithophyllum / Lithothamnion, Corallina spp, Barnacles, Actina equina and Nucella lapilus. On the sheltered sides there were many sponges (Halichondria, Hymeniacidon, Grantia) as well as Polysiphonia spp. and other filamentous reds. A large plateau extended beyond the ridges to LWST. Little rock was visible, being covered in encrusting red algae and Mastocarpus stellatus and kelp. The pools contained few boulders but there was an increased diversity of algae and fauna.

column2: Porifera has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. Rocky plateau with deep pools, fringed by Corallina, and Coralline crusts covering pool sides. Algae growth in pools sparse, often only on Patella. Deep gullies with vertical and overhanging sides harbouring many species in its fissured and creviced surface. Base of gullies scoured with sand/gravel and rock boulder base. More sheltered rockpools were rich in species and high in abundance. Anemones were common, particularly the beadlet. Sponges abundant. The survey reached MLWST, so only just into the Laminaria zone.

column2: Decapoda, Porifera has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. A broad gully, 10m wide, with coarse sand on bedrock with overlying large slate boulders (to 1.5m). Algae attached to boulders or bedrocks beneath sand. Sand tolerant species in abundance, including Polyides and Furcellaria. Underboulder very rich with near 100% cover by sponges, didemnids and bryozoans, including Diplosoma spongiforme, Trididemnum cereum and Ophlitaspongia seriata. Decapoda well represented as were fish. A rich habitat.***

column2: Mid-eulittoral pools in bedrock with afew loose boulders in the bottom. Sides of pools were dominated by Corallinacaea and Corallina officinalis with Mesophyllum lichenoides growing epiphytically. The bottoms of the pools supported a variety of brown algae, including Bifurcaria bifurcata, frequent Halidnrs siliquosa and Laminaria digitata. Large tufts of Calliblepharis jubata were the most common red alga. Fuana was sparse, with the pools supporting only anthozoa and an occasional Gibbula umbilicalis.

column3: Corallina has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. The rock platform was dissected by gullies running mainly in a SW direction and contained shallow pools with sandy bottoms. Corallina was abundant in the rock pools. The top-mid beach area of incised rock had deep rock pools with cobbles and little else. Many large Thick Topshells , Dogwhelks and eggs were found to be present. Keyhole limpets and cowries were also found. The wide rocky platform extended in both directions from the beach . Mesophyllum lichenoide was found on the corrallina in the shore rockpools.

The shore consisted of a fairly level, mica shist which contained many fissures and gullies, backed by mud cliffs. Rockpools had been formed in depressions on the surface. The area is characteristic for barnacle cover and encrusting red algae on drying and non-drying surfaces. Seaweed cover was poor, except at the lower shore where encrusting reds were dominant. The shore appeared to be quite exposed from all southerly directions (SW - ESE). This may make it unsuitable for crabs and other large mobile spp. Cthamalus montagui and Corallina in the pools were characteristic as were Patella, Nucella and Topshells.

column2: Rhodophycota indet.(non-calc.crusts) has been given a nominal abundance value of Present for this record as in Arev it had no abundance value. Hard slate with igneous intrusions forming high (to 5m) ridges, with many overhangs and vertical faces. Exposed seaward sides had much Lithophyllum / Lithothamnion, Corallina spp, Barnacles, Actina equina and Nucella lapilus. On the sheltered sides there were many sponges (Halichondria,H ymeniacidon, Grantia) as well as Polysiphonia spp. and other filamentous reds. A large plateau extended beyond the ridges to LWST. Little rock was visible, being covered in encrusting red algae and Mastocarpus stellatus and kelp. The pools contained few boulders but there was an increased diversity of algae and fauna.

column3: Porifera indet crusts has been given a nominal abundance value of Present for this record as in Arev it had no abundance value. Whole area surveyed. Upper/midshore typical exposed species. Ls deep pools, overhangs and surge gullies very interesting with a diverse flora and fauna of less commonly faunal species such as Halurus equisetifolius, Asparagopsis armata and superabundant Tubularia indivisa in the surge gullies. column3: Porifera has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. Rocky plateau with deep pools, fringed by Corallina, and Coralline crusts covering pool sides. Algae growth in pools sparse, often only on Patella. Deep gullies with vertical and overhanging sides harbouring many species in its fissured and creviced surface. Base of gullies scoured with sand/gravel and rock boulder base. More sheltered rockpools were rich in species and high in abundance. Anemones were common, particularly the beadlet. Sponges abundant. The survey reached MLWST, so only just into the Laminaria zone.

Red algae dominated rocks with abundant corallinaceae and Balanus perforatus. The low lying rocky shelf was incised by a series of narrow deep surge gullies, scoured by pebble. On severly scoured surfaces, Audouinella sp. and Enteromorphas p. were locally abundant. Undersurfaces also had a low turf of Lomentaria articulata and Callithamnion tetricum. Codium spp. was present in unusually high quantities. Kelp where present was sparse and grazed by abundant Helcion pellucidum.

column2: Supralittoral, predominatly upward facing bedrock, dominated by Caloplaca sp. Lecanora atra and indeterminate grey lickens. Ramalina sp. was abundant, particuarly growing out of fissures. There were several pools in this area, comepltely dominated by Enteromorpha.
column3: Shallow pools in a series of steps with occasional runs of water from one level to the next. Near 100% cover by Corallinacea, Actinia equina abundant as were Patella ulyssiponensis with Enteromorpha sp. attached. Bare rocks with sparse Fucus spiralis, Chthamalus and Littorina neritoides common. Ascophyllum nodosum appearing towards bottom of band.

column2: Patella ulyssiponensis has been given a nominal abundance value of Present for this record as in Arev it had no abundance value. Shallow gully rockpools in the mid eulittoral were dominated by Corallinaceae and Corallina officinalis with superabundant Patella ulyssiponensis. Fissures held Lipophrys pholis and Sagartia elegans. Limpet shells were covered in Enteromorpha spp.
column3: Slate and boulder filled gully just below shingle, running down the shore with through flowing water. Algae abundant, particularly Ceramium sp., Ulva and Chondrus etc. Sargassum in pools. Boulders with restricted underboulder community, relatively mobile and dominated by Spirorbidae. Corallinacea abundant, rock surfaces above water with abundant Fucoid and Ascophyllum growth.

column2: Decapoda, Porifera has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. A broad gully, 10m wide, with coarse sand on bedrock with overlying large slate boulders (to 1.5m). Algae attached to boulders or bedrocks beneath sand. Sand tolerant species in abundance, including Polyides and Furcellaria. Underboulder very rich with near 100% cover by sponges, didemnids and bryozoans, including Diplosoma spongiforme, Trididemnum cereum and Ophlitaspongia seriata. Decapoda well represented as were fish. A rich habitat.
column3: Lithophyllum has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. Bedrock of shists and igneous intrusions. Large rocky ridges and , near vertical faces with many overhangs, deep pools and fissures. Actina equina, Barnacles. Nucella lapillus and Patella vulgata were found on the exposed faces. Grantia compressa, Halichondria and Hymeniacion were found in shady places and overhangs with Plumaria elegans. Delesseria was found in shady places in deep pools. Opisthobranch were in fissures, as were many Cancer pagarus and Lipophrys pholis. Ridge adjacent to sandy bay and deep gully parallel to shoreline were surveyed.

column2: Exposed flat rocky shelf with many fissures and gullies. Rockpools formed in depressions. The shore is extremely exposed and this is reflected in the species found. Anemones were abundant as was coralline algae.
column3: A rugged bedrock plateau with numerous pools, gullies, overhangs and fissures. Restricted algal species and the majority of those present were not abundnant. Rockpools and gullies contained many boulders and some shelly gravel/sand. Actinia equina were abundant as were the corraline algae. In areas of heavy Patella grazing the Barnacles were dominant, particularly on the open, exposed faces. Richest areas for both species and abundance were the sheltered fissures and the vertical faces/overhangs.

column3: Rugged lower eulittoral bedrock with dense Mastocarpus stellatus interspersed with himanthalia elongata. Overhangs held a varied fauna includingGrantia compressa and a variety of sponges, together with frequent Aplidium punctum and Botrylus schlosseri.

column2: Decapoda, Porifera has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. A broad gully, 10m wide, with coarse sand on bedrock ith overlying large slate boulders (to 1.5m). Algae attached to boulders or bedrocks beneath sand. Sand tolerant species in abundance, including Polyides and Furcellaria. Underboulder very rich with near 100% cover by sponges, didemnids and bryozoans, including Diplosoma spongiforme, Trididemnum cereum and Ophlitaspongia seriata. Decapoda well represented as were fish. A rich habitat.

column2: Patella ulyssiponensis has been given a nominal abundance value of Present for this record as in Arev it had no abundance value. Shallow gully rockpools in the mid eulittoral were dominated by Corallinaceae and Corallina officinalis with superabundant Patella ulyssiponensis. Fissures held Lipophrys pholis and Sagartia elegans. Limpet shells were covered in Enteromorpha spp.
column3: Fissured rock with superabundant Littorina saxatilis. Pools shallow with Enteromorpha sp. and black blobs, plus other green algae. Gibbula umbilicalis occasional on rocks and Chthamalus rare.

column3: Lannacombe Beach West (Little Sleadon Rock to Langerstone Point)

column2: Rhodophycota indet.(non-calc.crusts) has been given a nominal abundance value of Present for this record as in Arev it had no abundance value. Hard slate with igneous intrusions forming high (to 5m) ridges, with many overhangs and vertical faces. Exposed seaward sides had much Lithophyllum / Lithothamnion, Corallina spp, Barnacles, Actina equina and Nucella lapilus. On the sheltered sides there were many sponges (Halichondria, Hymeniacidon, Grantia) as well as Polysiphonia spp. and other filamentous reds. A large plateau extended beyond the ridges to LWST. Little rock was visible, being covered in encrusting red algae and Mastocarpus stellatus and kelp. The pools contained few boulders but there was an increased diversity of algae and fauna.
column3: Porifera indet crusts has been given a nominal abundance value of Present for this record as in Arev it had no abundance value. Whole area surveyed. Upper/midshore typical exposed species. Ls deep pools, overhangs and surge gullies very interesting with a diverse flora and fauna of less commonly faunal species such as Halurus equisetifolius, Asparagopsis armata and superabundant Tubularia indivisa in the surge gullies. An excellent site with more to be found on a better tide.

column2: Shallow pools in a series of steps with occasional runs of water from one level to the next. Near 100% cover by Corallinacea, Actinia equina abundant as were Patella ulyssiponensis with Enteromorpha sp. attached. Bare rocks with sparse Fucus spiralis, Chthamalus and Littorina neritoides common. Ascophyllum nodosum appearing towards bottom of band.
column3: A rugged bedrock plateau with numerous pools, gullies, overhangs and fissures. Restricted algal species and the majority of those present were not abundnant. Rockpools and gullies contained many boulders and some shelly gravel/sand. Actinia equina were abundant as were the corraline algae. In areas of heavy Patella grazing the Barnacles were dominant, particularly on the open, exposed faces. Richest areas for both species and abundance were the sheltered fissures and the vertical faces/overhangs.

column2: A rugged bedrock plateau with numerous pools, gullies, overhangs and fissures. Restricted algal species and the majority of those present were not abundnant. Rockpools and gullies contained many boulders and some shelly gravel/sand. Actinia equina were abundant as were the corraline algae. In areas of heavy Patella grazing the Barnacles were dominant, particularly on the open, exposed faces. Richest areas for both species and abundance were the sheltered fissures and the vertical faces/overhangs.
column3: Lower eulittoral boulder-strewn pools with sand covering, dominated by various red algae. Coralline crusts and Corallina officinalis frequent around the sides of the pool, while Chondus crispus, Furcellaria lunbricalis and Himanthalia elongata were amoung those frequent in the bottom of pools, as was Ulva sp. Sand-tolerant species such as abundant Audouinella and Gracellaria verrucosa were also frequent. Small growths of Cystoseira were also present.
